headline: Rare Earth Processing in Pahang Draws RM14B Investment But Environmental Fears
context: Lynas Corporation's expanded rare earth processing facility in Gebeng, Pahang received RM14 billion in new investment commitments in 2025 to supply the global EV battery chain, while residents renewed protests over radioactive waste storage.

cards.big: RM14 billion rare earth investment versus 32,000 residents living within 15km of radioactive waste storage site
cards.sub: Lynas facility processes 28,000 tonnes of rare earth annually — producing 450,000 tonnes of low-level radioactive waste

cards.big: Rare earth processing contributes RM4.8 billion to GDP and positions Malaysia as critical mineral supplier for global EV market
cards.sub: China controls 90% of rare earth processing — Malaysia's facility provides strategic supply chain diversification value

cards.big: Kuantan residents report 12% higher incidence of respiratory illness within 5km radius per MOH epidemiological survey
cards.sub: Community compensation fund of RM28 million over 10 years amounts to RM88 per affected resident annually

cards.big: AELB granted operating licence renewal in 2023 despite Lynas missing 3 of 5 waste management deadlines
cards.sub: Permanent disposal facility for radioactive waste residue still not constructed 12 years after initial operating permit

cards.big: Strategic minerals matter — but storing radioactive waste permanently in a community that said no is environmental colonialism
Should Lynas be required to export all radioactive waste residue to Australia by 2028 or face licence revocation?
